talk_metadata.talk_title: Metabolic vulnerabilities of Nrf2‑active cancer cells
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we can't fully distinguish whether the labeled cystine entered as cystine or cysteine"
Speaker said: "we were surprised to find that knocking out both disulfide reductases only had a moderate effect on tumor number"
Speaker said: "we think that the microenvironment is very different in pancreas compared to lung"

key_data_gaps: No in vivo efficacy data for cystine transport inhibitors
No OS or tumor burden endpoints reported
Unclear contribution of macrophage‑derived cysteine in vivo

crispro_opportunity.description: Lung tumors show high interstitial cysteine but low cystine, suggesting cystine‑uptake inhibitors would be effective only in cystine‑dependent tumors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we would predict that if we blocked cystine uptake, this would be very potent to a lung tumor model, maybe less potent to a liver cancer model"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO could develop a predictive model linking tumor interstitial cysteine/cystine ratios to response to cystine‑transport blockade.

crispro_opportunity.description: Macrophage‑derived cysteine rescues erastin‑treated lung cancer cells, indicating a microenvironmental resistance mechanism.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "if we co‑culture our lung cancer cells with macrophages, it will rescue erastin treatment"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify combinatorial targets (e.g., cystine transport + macrophage cysteine secretion) to overcome this resistance.

crispro_opportunity.description: CB‑839 (glutaminase inhibitor) shows potency only in standard RPMI but not in tumor‑like interstitial fluid, suggesting a need for combination with agents that restore cystine dependence.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "in RPMI, cells become very sensitive to CB‑839, but in sTIF they really don't care"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model metabolic flux changes to predict synergistic drug pairs that re‑sensitize tumors to glutaminase inhibition.

unresolved_questions: What is the precise source of the high interstitial cysteine in lung tumors?
Can selective inhibition of GGT1 be achieved without immune neutralization?
How does the tumor microenvironment regulate the balance between cystine uptake and transsulfuration in different cancer types?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Mitochondrial protein degradation drives T‑cell exhaustion via regulatory heme
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "they start to degrade mitochondria protein to ensure they can still stay there"

Speaker said: "once you treat the cells with MG132, those T cell accumulate damaged mitochondria, they are terminal exhausted T cell. They express very low level of Bach2. But once you treat the MG132, you start to see Bach2 increase"
Speaker said: "we think that this degradation machinery can trigger the exhaustion commitment"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Proteasome activity in exhausted T cells
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Degradation of mitochondrial hemoproteins releases free heme that binds BACH2, driving exhaustion

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Proteasome inhibitor (bortezomib) use in T‑cell engineering
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker demonstrated that low‑dose bortezomib improves CAR‑T function, suggesting the platform is sensitive to proteasome modulation
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Current CAR‑T manufacturing does not incorporate proteasome modulation, potentially missing an efficacy lever

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that damaged mitochondria lead to increased proteasome‑mediated degradation of mitochondrial proteins, yet concluded that blocking proteasome activity (with bortezomib) restores stem‑like T‑cell phenotype, ignoring that proteasome inhibition could also impair antigen processing

key_data_gaps: Direct measurement of nuclear free heme levels
In vivo validation of BACH2‑heme binding as the causal link
Long‑term safety of proteasome inhibition in patients

crispro_opportunity.description: Targeting the heme‑BACH2 interaction could prevent exhaustion without global proteasome inhibition.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "when Heme become freely available, you shut down BACH2, you degrade BACH2, and then BLIMP1 will start to be expressed"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model heme‑binding pockets on BACH2 and screen for small molecules that block heme association.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine low‑dose bortezomib with checkpoint blockade to sustain progenitor‑exhausted T cells.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "prevent this entire proteasome activity to block mitochondria protein degradation process, you'll be able to reverse the T cell exhaustion pattern"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict synergistic dosing schedules that maximize stemness markers while minimizing toxicity.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design CAR‑T trials that stratify patients by baseline proteasome gene expression scores.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "response negatively associated with their proteasome gene expression score"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can generate a biomarker algorithm to select low‑proteasome‑activity donors for manufacturing.

cited_competitors.name: bortezomib
cited_competitors.context: used as a proteasome inhibitor to improve CAR‑T function

unresolved_questions: What are the downstream transcriptional programs activated by regulatory heme beyond BACH2?
Can selective inhibition of the heme‑chaperone PGRMC2 replicate the effects of proteasome inhibition?
What is the impact of chronic proteasome inhibition on antigen presentation in T cells?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Mitochondrial chaperone TREM1 regulates immunosuppressive tumor‑associated macrophages
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "when you delete TREM1, mitochondrial function become hyper"
Speaker said: "TIM‑4 activation suppresses TREM1 expression via AMPK‑MBK signaling"
Speaker said: "combination of TIM‑4 antibody and GMG3 inhibitor strongly reduces tumor burden"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Loss of TREM1 leads to hyperactive mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation that fuels immunosuppressive M2‑like phenotype

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: TIM‑4 blockade to restore TREM1 in TAMs
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No biomarker stratification for TIM‑4 high vs low macrophage populations
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential under‑powering due to heterogeneous TAM phenotypes

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker indicated that TIM‑4 blockade alone only modestly reduces immunosuppression, suggesting redundancy with other phosphatidylserine receptors
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Monotherapy with TIM‑4 antibody may be insufficient without concurrent metabolic or epigenetic targeting

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Efficacy depends on upstream TIM‑4 signaling; loss of TIM‑4 can diminish drug impact
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Requires combination to achieve durable tumor control

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that TREM1 loss increases mitochondrial activity yet concluded that restoring TREM1 will suppress oxidative phosphorylation, without addressing how TREM1 mechanistically restrains complex II activity

key_data_gaps: Lack of human clinical data for TIM‑4 or GMG3 targeting
No pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data for TREM1 modulation
Unclear safety profile of combined TIM‑4 antibody and GMG3 inhibitor

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a diagnostic assay for TIM‑4 high vs low TAMs to select patients for TIM‑4 antibody therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "tumor‑associated macrophages have high TIM‑4 expression level"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell RNA‑seq and imaging data to generate a TIM‑4 TAM score for patient enrollment.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine TIM‑4 blockade with GMG3 inhibition to synergistically reduce immunosuppressive TAM metabolism.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "combination treatment make numbers of tumor‑associated macrophage decrease and increase T cell infiltration"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model metabolic flux changes to predict optimal dosing ratios for the two agents.

crispro_opportunity.description: Repurpose existing TIM‑4 antibodies (originally developed for autoimmune disease) for oncology based on the presented mechanism.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "TIM‑4 monoclonal antibody shows same effect as genetic knockout"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can assess target engagement and off‑target risk in tumor versus normal tissue.

cited_competitors.name: CD47‑SIRPA blockade
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as a prominent macrophage checkpoint not covered in the talk

cited_competitors.name: CSF1R inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: cited as having limited clinical benefit

unresolved_questions: What is the direct molecular interaction between AMPK‑MBK signaling and TREM1 transcription?
Can selective activation of TREM1 be achieved without affecting other mitochondrial chaperones?
How does TIM‑4 mediated phosphatidylserine uptake quantitatively contribute to α‑KG production in TAMs?

assets_to_track: TREM1 modulators (if any in preclinical pipelines)
